mnemonics.title: Pointer to Member Syntax
mnemonics.content: Think of it as 'type CLASS_NAME::*pointer_name' - the class name sits between the data type and the asterisk, like a bridge connecting them.

mnemonics.title: Dot-Star and Arrow-Star
mnemonics.content: Use '.' when you have an object (obj.*ptr), use '->' when you have a pointer to object (objPtr->*ptr) - the arrow already implies pointer access.

acronyms.usage: int A::*ptr - declares ptr as pointer to int member of class A

acronyms.expansion: Dot-Star operator - dereference member pointer through object
Arrow-Star operator - dereference member pointer through object pointer
acronyms.usage: objPtr->*memberPtr accesses member through pointer to object

visualTips.visualization: Think of it as an offset into the class object. The pointer stores WHERE in the object the member is located, not the actual memory address.
Visualize as a combination of function address + 'this' pointer binding. It needs both to execute - the function code location AND which object to operate on.
The type includes class scope: 'int A::*' is a distinct type from 'int B::*' - they are incompatible even if both point to integers.

analogies.analogy: Like a house address with room number - the object is the house (base address), the member pointer is the room number (offset within the house). You need both to find the exact location.
Like using a key to open a door (. for physical key, -> for electronic keycard). Both achieve the same goal but different access methods.
Like a public notice board - it exists independently of any specific room (object), so you can access it directly without specifying which room.
